When this kanji is used as a single-character vocabulary word, it usually has the kun'yomi reading しな. You haven't seen the しな reading yet, so here's a mnemonic to help you remember it:

Data.MeaningMnemonic:
This is like a very formal and humble way of saying <vocabulary>to know</vocabulary> or <vocabulary>to believe</vocabulary>. Sure, the kanji means <kanji>suppose</kanji>, but think of it this way... When you say "I suppose" it's like a humble way of saying "I know" or "I believe." You're not being as direct about it this way, and in Japanese, that makes you more humble and polite. So, that's why 存じる means <vocabulary>to know</vocabulary> or <vocabulary>to believe</vocabulary>.

Note this word is typically used in the polite ます form. When you’re humble, you may want to be as polite as possible, I suppose!

ContextSentences.Ja: 地面にはたくさんの葉っぱが落ちていました。

ContextSentences.En: I'll take the dog for a short walk before the ground gets soaked from the rain.
ContextSentences.Ja: 雨で地面がビシャビシャになる前にワンコをちょっと歩かせてくるよ。

ContextSentences.En: Fugu said that he'd rather live underground than underwater.
ContextSentences.Ja: 海の中じゃなくて地面の中に住めたらいいのに、とフグは言いました。

Data.MeaningMnemonic: The <kanji>ground</kanji>/<kanji>earth</kanji> <kanji>face</kanji> is the surface that makes up the <vocabulary>ground</vocabulary> or the <vocabulary>Earth's surface</vocabulary>.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: The reading for 面 is what you'd expect. 地 on the other hand is じ, which is a bit different (though close sounding) to what you learned previously. Thank the Chinese and their ever changing language for this one. Think of <reading>Je</reading>sus (じ) laying on the ground, telling you how comfortable the ground is. You say "Jesus, you have to get off the ground, you have a dinner to go to," but he doesn't want to leave.

ContextSentences.En: You shouldn’t be fooled by her outward appearance.
ContextSentences.Ja: 彼女の外面にだまされない方がいいですよ。

ContextSentences.En: He has a good outward appearance and always smiles in public.
ContextSentences.Ja: 彼は外面が良く、人前ではいつも笑顔を見せています。

ContextSentences.En: These glasses have a flat curvature on the outer surface of the lens, so there's less distortion and glare in the peripheral areas.
ContextSentences.Ja: このメガネは、レンズの外面のカーブをフラットにしているので、周辺部のボヤケやユガミが少ないんです。

Data.MeaningMnemonic:
The <kanji>outside</kanji> <kanji>face</kanji> to something is the <vocabulary>external appearance</vocabulary> of that thing.

While 外面 can refer to the outer surface of an object, it’s more commonly used to describe someone's <vocabulary>outward appearance</vocabulary>, behavior, or demeanor on the <vocabulary>surface</vocabulary>.
Data.ReadingMnemonic:
This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji, so this is がいめん. But note that this can also use the kun’yomi readings, which makes it そとづら.

While がいめん is neutral, そとづら is a bit negative, often implying that the person's outward appearance might be deceptive, hiding their true nature.

ContextSentences.En: If you stare at computer screens for a long time, your eyesight will get worse.
ContextSentences.Ja: ずっとコンピューターの画面ばかり見ていると目が悪くなるよ。

ContextSentences.En: Can I ask you to turn down the screen brightness a bit?
ContextSentences.Ja: 画面の明るさをもう少し下げてもらってもいいですか？

ContextSentences.En: Lately, images of dogs often pop up on my screen automatically.
ContextSentences.Ja: 最近、よく犬の画像が自動で画面に出てくるんです。

Data.MeaningMnemonic: The <kanji>image</kanji> <kanji>surface</kanji> is a <vocabulary>screen</vocabulary>. Just imagine your computer screen. There's an image on the surface, right? It just happens to move around all the time, like most images on screens these days.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. If you know the readings of your kanji you'll know how to read this as well.

ContextSentences.En: I have something to talk to you about face-to-face. When are you available?
ContextSentences.Ja: あなたに、面と向かってお話したいことがあります。ご予定は、いつが空いていますか？

Data.MeaningMnemonic: The kanji and the word are exactly the same. That means they share meanings as well.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: The reading is the same as what you learned with the kanji.

ContextSentences.En: That was the funniest thing I’ve ever heard.
ContextSentences.Ja: それは今まで聞いた中で、一番面白い話でした。

ContextSentences.En: This manga is a bit crude but it's very interesting.
ContextSentences.Ja: このマンガは、ちょっと下品だけどとても面白いんです。

ContextSentences.En: He is an amusing guy, his only downside is that he gets angry over the smallest things.
ContextSentences.Ja: あいつは面白いやつだけど、ちょっとしたことですぐ切れるのがネックだよな。

Data.MeaningMnemonic: The <kanji>face</kanji> of this object is so <kanji>white</kanji>! Why is it so white compared to everything else? This is so <vocabulary>interesting</vocabulary>...
Data.ReadingMnemonic: The reading is unlike what you've learned before, at least with the 面 part (白い is just like the vocab word 白い). When you look at the white face of the object you say to your friend Moe: "<reading>Oh mo</reading>!" (おも) because you want him to come over and look at this. Try to imagine someone you know named Moe, or at least a celebrity or something named Moe.

ContextSentences.En: I found a new book at the library.
ContextSentences.Ja: 図書館で新しい本を見つけました。

ContextSentences.En: Library use is free of charge.
ContextSentences.Ja: 図書館の利用にお金はかかりませんよ。

ContextSentences.En: I like studying at the library.
ContextSentences.Ja: 私は図書館で勉強するのが好きなんです。

Data.MeaningMnemonic: A <kanji>public building</kanji> where <kanji>writte</kanji>n things are all <kanji>diagram</kanji>ed out in a way that lets you find said written things? That sounds like a <vocabulary>library</vocabulary> to me!
Data.ReadingMnemonic: The 書館 part should be readable by you, but it's the 図 that's troublesome with an alternate reading from the one you learned. Imagine yourself at the library. You check out every single book you can about the human <reading>toe</reading> (と). You have a giant stack of books on it, then you drop it... ironically on your toe. You decide to <reading>show Khan</reading> (your buddy) your smashed toe.

ContextSentences.En: He is a world-renowned chef.
ContextSentences.Ja: 彼は世界的に有名な料理人です。

ContextSentences.En: My girlfriend works as a cook in a restaurant.
ContextSentences.Ja: 僕の彼女は料理人としてレストランで働いています。

ContextSentences.En: Chefs also need to pay attention to the presentation of their dishes.
ContextSentences.Ja: 料理人は料理のプレゼンテーションにも気を配らなくてはなりません。

Data.MeaningMnemonic: You've seen 料理 and probably know it means "cooking." So, what is a <kanji>person</kanji> who does cooking? That is <vocabulary>a cook</vocabulary> or <vocabulary>chef</vocabulary>.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. If you know the readings of your kanji you'll know how to read this as well.

ContextSentences.En: He isn't a famous artist.
ContextSentences.Ja: 彼は有名な芸術家ではありません。

ContextSentences.En: She is a globally renowned artist who has received numerous awards.
ContextSentences.Ja: 彼女は、多くの賞を受賞している、世界的に名の知れた芸術家です。

ContextSentences.En: The artist asked his disciple to look after his house while he was away and left for Europe to study.
ContextSentences.Ja: その芸術家は、弟子に留守番をたのんで、ヨーロッパに留学しました。

Data.MeaningMnemonic: You know that 芸術 is <kanji>art</kanji> so you'd think that 芸術家 would be something like "art house." Well, it's not. Think of it this way: Who is the person who works for "House Art"? It's the <vocabulary>artist</vocabulary>.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. If you know the readings of your kanji you'll know how to read this as well.

ContextSentences.En: This train's doors don't open automatically.
ContextSentences.Ja: この電車のドアは、自動的には開きません。

ContextSentences.En: If that country starts a war, our country will automatically be drawn into the conflict as well.
ContextSentences.Ja: あの国が戦争を始めると、俺たちの国も自動的に戦うことになるんだよ。

ContextSentences.En: The data automatically backed up here is managed by a system that will also automatically delete it after a certain period of time.
ContextSentences.Ja: こちらに自動的にバックアップされたデータは、今度は一定期間後に自動的に消去されるシステムになっています。

ContextSentences.En: This is due to an automatic logout after more than 30 minutes of inactivity.
ContextSentences.Ja: これは、30分以上何もしなかった場合の自動的なログアウトによるものですね。

Data.MeaningMnemonic: An <kanji>automatic</kanji><kanji>al</kanji> (自動 + 的) thing of course isn't "automatical." A better way to say it is to cut off the -al and call it <vocabulary>automatic</vocabulary>.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. If you know the readings of your kanji you'll know how to read this as well. It will help to know the reading of 自動 as well.

ContextSentences.En: I generally don't believe in spiritual matters.
ContextSentences.Ja: 私は基本的にはスピリチュアルな事は信じません。

ContextSentences.En: I was told by my boss that I don't even know the fundamentals and that I'm useless.
ContextSentences.Ja: 上司に、基本的な事も知らない役立たずだと言われました。

ContextSentences.En: Basically, I have no interest in politics.
ContextSentences.Ja: 基本的に政治に関心がないんです。

Data.MeaningMnemonic: The things that are <kanji>basics</kanji> (基本) <kanji>like</kanji> (的) are <vocabulary>basic</vocabulary> and <vocabulary>fundamental</vocabulary>.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. If you know the readings of your kanji you'll know how to read this as well. Knowing how to read the word 基本 will be helpful too.

ContextSentences.En: He reads books without ever creasing the pages.
ContextSentences.Ja: 彼はゼッタイに折り目をつけずに本を読みます。

ContextSentences.En: Please open the origami again after you fold it in half to add the crease.
ContextSentences.Ja: 折り紙を半分に折って、折り目をつけてからまた開いてください。

ContextSentences.En: Could you put a crease in my trousers?
ContextSentences.Ja: ズボンには折り目を付けてもらえますか？

Data.MeaningMnemonic: A <kanji>fold</kanji> you can see with your <kanji>eye</kanji> is a <vocabulary>fold</vocabulary> or <vocabulary>crease</vocabulary> in something.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: The readings are both kun'yomi vocab readings. You should have seen 折る by now which should help you to read 折り. Then there's 目, which I'm guessing you won't have a problem with.

ContextSentences.En: Tomorrow is my graduation ceremony.
ContextSentences.Ja: 明日は私の卒業式です。

ContextSentences.En: Ah, this is a photo from after the graduation ceremony, right? How nostalgic!
ContextSentences.Ja: あ、これ、卒業式の後の写真だね。なつかしい！

ContextSentences.En: Huh? Did you say that Beyoncé came to the graduation and sang for you guys? No way! You must be joking!
ContextSentences.Ja: はあ？卒業式にビヨンセが来て歌を歌ってくれた？まさか！冗談でしょう？

Data.MeaningMnemonic: You know that 卒業 is <kanji>graduation</kanji> so what about when you add <kanji>ceremony</kanji> to it? That makes it a <vocabulary>graduation ceremony</vocabulary>.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. If you know the readings of your kanji you'll know how to read this as well. It will help if you know how to read 卒業 already as a word, too.

ContextSentences.En: Everyone is imperfect.
ContextSentences.Ja: 人間はみんな不完全です。

ContextSentences.En: Aren't incomplete things better than complete things, since they have a humanness about them?
ContextSentences.Ja: 完全なものよりも不完全なものの方が人情味があってよくない？

ContextSentences.En: The Tofugu softball team had an incomplete end to their season when the game they were winning was suspended due to rain.
ContextSentences.Ja: 勝ち試合が雨で中止になり、トーフグソフトボールチームのシーズンが不完全に終わった。

Data.MeaningMnemonic: You know that 完全 means "complete" or "perfect." So, a <kanji>not</kanji> "complete" or "perfect" is something that is <vocabulary>incomplete</vocabulary> or <vocabulary>imperfect</vocabulary>.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. If you know the readings of your kanji you'll know how to read this as well. It also will help if you can read 完全.

ContextSentences.En: I went to an art museum workshop.
ContextSentences.Ja: 美術館のワークショップに行ってきました。

ContextSentences.En: We had an art museum date over the weekend.
ContextSentences.Ja: 週末は美術館デートでした。

ContextSentences.En: When I went to the Louvre Museum in Paris, I saw famous artworks like the "Mona Lisa."
ContextSentences.Ja: パリのルーヴル美術館に行った時に「モナリザ」などの有名なアート作品を見ました。

Data.MeaningMnemonic: You know 美術 is <kanji>fine art</kanji>. So what is a <kanji>public building</kanji> that contains said fine art? It's a <vocabulary>museum</vocabulary>.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. If you know the readings of your kanji you'll know how to read this as well.

ContextSentences.En: I'm a professional origami artist.
ContextSentences.Ja: 私はプロの折り紙アーティストです。

ContextSentences.En: I stockpiled everyday items, cosmetics, groceries, and origami paper during the Amazon sale.
ContextSentences.Ja: Amazonのセールで、日用品、コスメ、食料品、折り紙を買いだめしました。

ContextSentences.En: Today, Koichi found origami instead of food in his bento box.
ContextSentences.Ja: 今日のコウイチの弁当箱の中には、なぜか食べ物の代わりに折り紙が入っていました。

Data.MeaningMnemonic: You know that 折る is "to fold," so what about "to fold <kanji>paper</kanji>"? Ever heard of something that involves lots of folding of paper that is Japanese? Most likely you have. This is <vocabulary>origami</vocabulary>, aka the <vocabulary>art of paper folding</vocabulary>.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: The reading comes from 折る and the vocab word 紙. As long as you know those you should be able to read this. Also, you probably already know this reading because it's an English word, too.

ContextSentences.En: I hope you can get into the university of your choice!
ContextSentences.Ja: 希望する大学に入学できるといいですね！

ContextSentences.En: I'm hoping to study abroad in Japan.
ContextSentences.Ja: 日本への留学を希望しています。

ContextSentences.En: I'll do my best to schedule shifts for you according to your preferences, but sometimes adjustments may be necessary if everyone's preferences don't align. Thank you for your understanding in this matter.
ContextSentences.Ja: できるだけ希望するシフトでバイトに入ってもらえるようにしますが、みんなの希望が合わない時は調整が必要なこともあるので、そこはよろしくお願いします。

Data.MeaningMnemonic:
You know that 希望 is a "wish." When you "do a wish," that means <vocabulary>to wish</vocabulary>, <vocabulary>to request</vocabulary> or <vocabulary>to hope</vocabulary>.

This is a formal way to say that you wish for something, so you might use it at work to request an opportunity to work abroad, or let them know politely that you're resigning.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: The reading comes from 希望. It's also the on'yomi jukugo reading as well, if that helps any.

ContextSentences.En: I'm not a doctor, but I learned surgical techniques from a doctor.
ContextSentences.Ja: 私は医者ではありませんが、ある医者から、手術の技術を学びました。

ContextSentences.En: I learned the latest web development skills from the company I used to work for.
ContextSentences.Ja: 以前に働いていた会社で、ウエブ開発の最新技術を身につけました。

ContextSentences.En: During the presentation, a Google engineer suddenly stood up and started singing karaoke.
ContextSentences.Ja: プレゼン中、グーグルの技術者が急に立ち上がり、カラオケを始めたんです。

Data.MeaningMnemonic: You've seen 技 as a vocab word meaning "technique," and the kanji 術 means <kanji>technique</kanji>. So this means <vocabulary>technique</vocabulary>! Your technique is also your <vocabulary>craft</vocabulary>, and what’s needed for <vocabulary>technology</vocabulary>.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. If you know the readings of your kanji you'll know how to read this as well.

ContextSentences.En: I'll compromise on 100,000 yen.
ContextSentences.Ja: 十万円で妥協するよ。

ContextSentences.En: Koichi and Viet compromised by going to McDonald's.
ContextSentences.Ja: コウイチとビエトは、マクドナルドに行くことで妥協しました。

ContextSentences.En: After all, you don't want to compromise on the design and interior of your own home, right?
ContextSentences.Ja: やっぱり、自分の家のデザインやインテリアは妥協したくないじゃん。

Data.MeaningMnemonic: You know that 妥協 means <kanji>compromise</kanji>. So, what happens when you make it a verb? It becomes <vocabulary>to compromise</vocabulary>.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: The reading is the same as the vocab word 妥協, so if you know that you should be good (also, just the reading of the kanji works too).

ContextSentences.En: What if I told you that I was planning to graduate from this industry soon?
ContextSentences.Ja: この業界、そろそろ卒業するつもりなんだって言ったらどうする？

ContextSentences.En: I can't wait to finish school, but I don't want to grow up.
ContextSentences.Ja: 学校は早く卒業したいけど、大人にはなりたくないな。

ContextSentences.En: Is it possible to graduate from art school or an art college and still not find a job in art?
ContextSentences.Ja: 美大や芸大を卒業しても、アートの仕事が見つからないこともあるんですか？

Data.MeaningMnemonic: You know that 卒業 is "graduation." Make it a verb, and you have <vocabulary>to graduate</vocabulary>.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: The reading is just the same as the vocab word 卒業.

ContextSentences.En: It is not good to leave behind food.
ContextSentences.Ja: 食べ物を残すのは良くないですよ。

ContextSentences.En: I left a comment on your blog.
ContextSentences.Ja: ブログにコメントを残しました。

ContextSentences.En: I intend to be a big name in the world someday.
ContextSentences.Ja: いつか世に名を残せるような大物になるつもりです。

Data.MeaningMnemonic: This word consists of kanji with hiragana attached. Because the hiragana ends with an う sound, you know this word is a verb. The kanji itself means <kanji>remainder</kanji>, but you can't really "to remainder." What is something that's similar that would make sense? How about <vocabulary>to leave behind</vocabulary>. This is when you're actually doing the leaving behind too. For example, you eat some food and you leave behind your rice because you're a bad child. You can remember this because of す. Every time you leave some food behind your mom would <reading>sue</reading> you, because she was a terrible mom. Imagine this.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: The reading for this word can come from level 14 item 残る. Associate the two in your mind and you'll be able to read both.

ContextSentences.En: This is a recipe for a rare cheese cake that hardens in the microwave without gelatin or flame.
ContextSentences.Ja: これはゼラチンも火も使わずにレンジでチンして固めるレアチーズケーキのレシピです。

ContextSentences.En: Viet takes an hour to make his pompadour hard every morning.
ContextSentences.Ja: ビエトは毎朝一時間かけてリーゼントをワックスで固めます。

ContextSentences.En: Koichi was determined to make WaniKani the best Japanese language learning site in the world.
ContextSentences.Ja: コウイチは、ワニカニを世界一の日本語学習サイトにする決意を固めた。

Data.MeaningMnemonic: This word consists of kanji with hiragana attached. Because the hiragana ends with an う sound, you know this word is a verb. The kanji itself means <kanji>hard</kanji> so the verb vocab version? <vocabulary>To make something hard</vocabulary>. You need to differentiate this from 固まる, though, which means "to harden" (the hardening happens on its own). This word means "to make something hard," because you're doing the hardening. You can remember this because you want to make something hard. And to do that, you send special "<reading>mail</reading>" (める) to this thing you want to make hard, and this mail does that for you.
Data.ReadingMnemonic:
Since the reading for 固 (かた) is different than the one you learned with the kanji, here's a mnemonic to help you:

Why would you want <vocabulary>to make something hard</vocabulary>? Obviously because you want to put it in a <reading>cata</reading>pult (かた). There's no point putting soft things in catapults, after all.

ContextSentences.En: Do you have something to verify your identity?
ContextSentences.Ja: 何か身分を証明できる物は持っていますか？

ContextSentences.En: I need a certified photo with date stamp.
ContextSentences.Ja: 日付スタンプのある証明写真が必要なんです。

ContextSentences.En: Please show me proof that you have finished up to level sixty of WaniKani. If you don't prove it, I won't believe you.
ContextSentences.Ja: ワニカニのレベル60まで終わったっていう証明を見せてください。証明がないなら信じませんよ。

Data.MeaningMnemonic: Is the <kanji>evidence</kanji> <kanji>bright</kanji> enough to convince me? Is this really <vocabulary>proof</vocabulary> enough to <vocabulary>prove</vocabulary> it?
Data.ReadingMnemonic:
This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. You didn't learn the on'yomi for 明 (めい) with the kanji, so here's a mnemonic to help you remember it:

You need to show me <vocabulary>proof</vocabulary> to make me believe in the month of <reading>May</reading> (めい). Does it even exist? It may or may not be real (that's why it's called May), so you have to <vocabulary>prove</vocabulary> it to me somehow.

ContextSentences.En: What day of the week is today?
ContextSentences.Ja: 今日って何曜日だっけ？

ContextSentences.En: I have Japanese lessons on Fridays.
ContextSentences.Ja: 金曜日は、日本語のレッスンがあります。

ContextSentences.En: At our place, we've decided on a set dinner menu for each day of the week
ContextSentences.Ja: うちの家では、曜日ごとに夕飯のメニューを決めてるんです。

Data.MeaningMnemonic: The <kanji>weekday</kanji> <kanji>day</kanji> is the <vocabulary>day of the week</vocabulary>, as in "Monday" or "Tuesday." You'll be learning those soon, but first you should learn 曜日 to get you prepared.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: This word uses the on'yomi reading for 曜 and the kun'yomi reading for 日. You've learned both of these already, but be careful because ひ of 日 changes to び here.

ContextSentences.En: I was born in Osaka.
ContextSentences.Ja: 私は生まれは大阪です。

ContextSentences.En: Osaka has a lot of delicious food.
ContextSentences.Ja: 大阪には美味しい食べ物がたくさんあります。

ContextSentences.En: I was so excited about the Osaka trip that I totally neglected my studies.
ContextSentences.Ja: 大阪旅行が楽しみすぎて、勉強がすっかりお留守になってしまっていました。

Data.MeaningMnemonic: A <kanji>big</kanji> place called <kanji>Osaka</kanji>? This is the city of <vocabulary>Osaka</vocabulary>! Ever heard of it? It's a big city, and kind of the rival to Tokyo.
Data.ReadingMnemonic:
The おおさか reading for this word is made up of the kun'yomi for each kanji. You've learned the おお reading (like in 大きい), but you haven't seen the さか reading for 阪 yet. 

That said, doesn't 阪 look a lot like 坂, which also means "slope"? Turns out they share the さか reading as well, so if you remember the word 坂 you can apply that reading here. (In fact, 大阪 was sometimes written as 大坂 in the old days.) The easiest mnemonic is probably just to think of the city of  <vocabulary>Osaka</vocabulary> though… Just don't forget about the double おお.

ContextSentences.En: He's not interested in others, you know.
ContextSentences.Ja: アイツは他人に関心がないんだよ。

ContextSentences.En: In our case, my father had absolutely zero concern in parenting.
ContextSentences.Ja: うちの場合は、父親の子育てへの関心がゼロだったんですよね。

ContextSentences.En: Everyone, please take more interest in politics.
ContextSentences.Ja: 皆さん、もっと政治に関心を持ってください。

Data.MeaningMnemonic: The things <kanji>related</kanji> to your <kanji>heart</kanji> are the things that <vocabulary>concern</vocabulary> and <vocabulary>interest</vocabulary> you the most.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. If you know the readings of your kanji you'll know how to read this as well.

ContextSentences.En: That's a really great plan!
ContextSentences.Ja: すごくいい企画ですね！

ContextSentences.En: I'm currently working on planning the Christmas event.
ContextSentences.Ja: 今、クリスマスイベントの企画を進めてるんだ。

ContextSentences.En: It was a plan I worked hard on, but it got scrapped.

Data.MeaningMnemonic: Remember how 生まれる means "to be born"? This word is similar, but it's used for when non-living things are "born". So as an intransitive verb, this means <vocabulary>to occur</vocabulary> or <vocabulary>to arise</vocabulary>. When used transitively, it means <vocabulary>to cause something</vocabulary>. You'll probably encounter the intransitive use much more, though.
Data.ReadingMnemonic:
You'd think this would use kun'yomi, but it actually doesn't! Like other verbs ending in じる, this takes the on'yomi reading of the kanji. 生 has two of those, so here's a mnemonic to help you remember which one to use: 

If you wanted anything <vocabulary>to occur</vocabulary> in medieval Japan, you needed to talk to the <reading>Shou</reading>gun. You always needed the Shougun's help <vocabulary>to cause something</vocabulary>. Without the Shougun, nothing would ever happen!

ContextSentences.En: I found a ramen restaurant that has a line.
ContextSentences.Ja: 行列のできるラーメン屋を見つけた。

ContextSentences.En: Oh, a line of ants!
ContextSentences.Ja: あ、アリの行列だ！

ContextSentences.En: I lined up in a queue at midnight to buy concert tickets.
ContextSentences.Ja: コンサートのチケットを買うために、夜中から行列にならびました。

ContextSentences.En: I learned about matrices today in math class.
ContextSentences.Ja: 今日は数学のクラスで行列について学びました。

Data.MeaningMnemonic:
You know the two words that make up this word, 行 and 列, and they both have similar meanings: a line and a row. Put them together and you have a <vocabulary>line</vocabulary> or a <vocabulary>queue</vocabulary>.

This usually means a line of people waiting for their turn, for example to enter a restaurant. It can be also used for a line that marches, like a line in a parade. In a mathematics context, it can refer to a <vocabulary>matrix</vocabulary>, which makes sense because they're lines and rows of numbers and stuff.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: This word takes the readings of the two words that make it up, so you should be lining up to read it!

ContextSentences.En: It is dangerous to stack so many plates.
ContextSentences.Ja: そんなにお皿を重ねると危ないですよ。

ContextSentences.En: The princess became more and more beautiful as she grew older.
ContextSentences.Ja: プリンセスは、年を重ねるごとに美しくなっていきました。

ContextSentences.En: Isn't it a waste of time to have a bunch of discussions about things that don't have answers?
ContextSentences.Ja: 答えのないことにディスカッションを重ねるのってムダじゃないですか？

Data.MeaningMnemonic:
You've got lots of <kanji>heavy</kanji> things, so the only logical thing is <vocabulary>to stack</vocabulary> them all up. 

Besides heavy physical things, you can also use this word when you're going <vocabulary>to accumulate</vocabulary> things like experience, practice, or years on your back.
Data.ReadingMnemonic:
Since this word consists of a kanji with hiragana attached, you can bet that it will use the kun'yomi reading. You didn't learn that reading with this kanji, so here's a mnemonic to help you:

All the things you've <vocabulary>stack</vocabulary>ed up were in preparation for your new <reading>casa</reading> (かさ). With everything inside boxes and neatly piled up, you're finally ready to move into your new casa. Hooray!

AuxiliaryMeanings.Meaning: To Put On Top Of Another

ContextSentences.En: Hey, isn't that former Prime Minister Mori?
ContextSentences.Ja: あれ、森元首相じゃない？

ContextSentences.En: What do you think about the Prime Minister's remarks yesterday?
ContextSentences.Ja: 昨日の首相の発言について、どう思いますか？

ContextSentences.En: This is where the Prime Minister can really show off their leadership, isn't it?
ContextSentences.Ja: これは首相のリーダーシップの見せ所ですね。

Data.MeaningMnemonic: The <kanji>neck</kanji> <kanji>minister</kanji>? This meaning <vocabulary>prime minister</vocabulary> makes sense if you think about the neck being the most important part of the body, kind of like the head!
Data.ReadingMnemonic:
This is a jukugo word that uses the on'yomi readings of the kanji. It also uses a reading for 相 that you haven't learned yet, so here's a mnemonic to help you:

The first <vocabulary>prime minister</vocabulary>s of Japan were actually the <reading>sho</reading>guns (しょう)… kind of.

ContextSentences.En: Please come again when the new greenery is out.
ContextSentences.Ja: 新緑の時期にまた来てくださいね。

ContextSentences.En: April and May are definitely the months when the new foliage is most beautiful.
ContextSentences.Ja: 新緑が一番きれいなのはやっぱり四月と五月ですよ。

ContextSentences.En: I feel like my eyesight improved just from gazing at the mountains covered in new green leaves.
ContextSentences.Ja: 新緑の山をずーっと見てたら目が良くなった気がするんだ。

Data.MeaningMnemonic:
What's <kanji>new</kanji> and <kanji>green</kanji>? That'd be the <vocabulary>new greenery</vocabulary> you see on trees and other plants every year!

新緑 refers to <vocabulary>new foliage</vocabulary> — fresh green leaves of late spring and early summer, just after they've fully emerged. In Japan, it's seen as a symbol of that time of year, so it often carries a slightly poetic nuance.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. You've seen them before (remember the りょく reading from 緑茶?), so you should be good to go here.

ContextSentences.En: The next train is in 10 minutes.
ContextSentences.Ja: 次の列車は10分後です。

ContextSentences.En: The train slowly began to move.
ContextSentences.Ja: 列車はゆっくりと動き出しました。

ContextSentences.En: Have you ever taken a night train?
ContextSentences.Ja: 夜行列車に乗ったことはありますか？

ContextSentences.En: When the train entered the tunnel, only the sound grew louder.
ContextSentences.Ja: 列車がトンネルに入ると、音だけが大きくなった。

Data.MeaningMnemonic:
You see a <kanji>row</kanji> of <kanji>car</kanji>s lined up neatly. Are they stuck in traffic? Nope. They're train cars. Congratulations, you've found a <vocabulary>train</vocabulary>!

You learned 電車 means "train," but it technically just refers to electric ones. On the other hand, 列車 is the broader, all-trains-welcome term. Diesel? Steam? Fancy long-distance express? All covered. But 列車 sounds more formal, so while 電車 is an everyday word, you may come across 列車 more often in writing or in the official descriptions used by railway companies.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. If you know the readings of your kanji you'll know how to read this as well. Just be sure to note the れつ reading, which gets changed to れっ.
